1: Moses and the elders speak together — the only place in Deuteronomy where Moses delivers a charge jointly with the elders (ziqnê yiśrāʾēl). This signals the emerging transfer of leadership to the community's elder structure post-Moses. The charge is simple: "Keep every commandment I am giving you today." The ceremony ahead is the formal covenant-entry liturgy for the land.
2: Large stones coated with plaster (wĕśadtā ʾōtām baśśîḏ) — a writing surface for public inscription. The plastered-stone technique is attested archaeologically: the Deir Alla inscription (8th century, Jordan) used plastered walls for text. The command is for the day of Jordan crossing — the first act in the land is the public display of the covenant law, not military operations or territorial consolidation.
3: "Write on these stones all the words of this law when you cross over." The Deuteronomic torah is not a priestly secret; it is publicly displayed on monumental stones at the entry point of the land. The repeated formula "land flowing with milk and honey" and the ancestral promise anchor the law in the covenant history — the law is not an abstract legal code but the terms of the relationship with the God who brought them there.
4: Mount Ebal — strikingly, the plastered stones are set up on the mountain of cursing rather than the mountain of blessing (Gerizim). The law stands on the mountain of sanctions, not the mountain of rewards. The law's primary horizon is the curse-sanction that enforces covenant faithfulness. Joshua 8:30–35 records the actual fulfillment of this ceremony after the initial Canaanite campaigns.
5: The altar of uncut stones: "an altar to the LORD your God out of stones that no iron tool has touched." ʾăḇānîm šĕlēmôt (complete/whole stones) — the prohibition of iron tools on the altar echoes Exodus 20:25. The rationale remains debated: some see a distinction between sacred/natural materials and human technological manufacture; others suggest that iron implements, associated with weapons and warfare, were ritually inappropriate for the altar of the God of life. The uncut stones make an aniconic, unadorned altar — no sculpted imagery, no craft enhancement.
6: Burnt offerings on the uncut altar — ʿōlôt, wholly consumed offerings ascending entirely to YHWH. The first liturgical act at the altar of the new land is total consecration: nothing is retained, everything is surrendered to YHWH. The land is entered under the sign of complete devotion before it is claimed as a place of human settlement.
7: Peace offerings and eating follow the burnt offerings — šĕlāmîm, the communion/fellowship offering where portions are shared among the worshippers. The covenantal feast follows the covenantal oath. "Rejoicing in the presence of the LORD your God" is the experiential content of covenant entry: the relationship with YHWH is not merely obligatory but celebratory. The meal at the altar unites the people with their God and with each other.
8: "Write all the words of this law on the stones very clearly" — bāʾēr hēṭēḇ, "explain/make plain thoroughly." The inscription must be legible and comprehensible to the entire people. This is the Deuteronomic democratization of the law: the covenant document is not lodged in the temple's inner sanctuary for priestly access only but displayed publicly for all to read. The word bāʾēr (to explain, make clear) connects to the title "Deuteronomy" (the LXX translates Deuteronomy 17:18 as to deuteronomion touto, "this second law," from this root's connotation of plain re-statement).
9: "Be silent, Israel, and listen" (hasskēt ûšĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) — a solemn call to attention from Moses and the Levitical priests together. Then the declaration: "Today you have become the people of the LORD your God." The Jordan crossing is not merely geographic; it is covenantal. The moment of entering the land is the moment of formally becoming YHWH's covenant people at the site of the law's promulgation. The Ebal ceremony enacts this constitutive identity.
10: "Obey the LORD your God by keeping his commandments and statutes that I am giving you today." Becoming YHWH's people (Deuteronomy 27:9) and obeying YHWH's law (Deuteronomy 27:10) are inseparable. The identity precedes the obligation — "you have become his people, therefore obey" — not "obey in order to become his people." Covenant membership is the foundation of covenant ethics, not its reward.
11: "On that same day Moses gave this command." The ceremony is immediate and specific — not a vague future obligation but a command tied to the specific day of covenant entry. The urgency of "that same day" underlines that covenant ratification cannot be deferred once the threshold of the land has been crossed.
12: Six tribes on Mount Gerizim for blessing: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin. These are primarily the sons of Leah (Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun) and Rachel (Joseph = Ephraim + Manasseh, Benjamin), minus Zebulun. The tribal arrangement for blessing and curse has generated extensive discussion; the division is not entirely genealogical. Gerizim stands south of the valley between the mountains; the natural amphitheater effect would allow voices to carry across.
13: Six tribes on Mount Ebal for the curse: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali. Four of these are sons of the maidservants Bilhah and Zilpah (Gad, Asher, Dan, Naphtali); Reuben is Leah's eldest but was disgraced (Genesis 35:22, sleeping with Bilhah); Zebulun is Leah's. The curse-side tribes are those with marginalized or complicated status in the ancestral narratives. Joshua 8:33 confirms the ceremony was enacted after Ai.
14: The Levites call out in a loud voice (qôl rām) — the curse-pronouncements are not whispered legal penalties but liturgical proclamations heard by the entire assembly. The Levites serve as the covenant-mediating tribe, the voice of YHWH's sanctions among the people. The public character of the Amen-response makes the entire community complicit in covenant accountability: everyone hears, everyone responds, everyone is bound.
15: First curse: secret idolatry — "whoever carves or casts an idol... and sets it up in secret" (bassāter). The first commandment's prohibition on images is here applied specifically to hidden image-worship. Public apostasy was liable to judicial sanction (Deuteronomy 17:2–7); this curse fills the gap for secret sins that human courts cannot reach. The congregational Amen transforms the curse-formula into a covenantal oath: the people actively invoke the curse upon anyone — including themselves — who commits this hidden sin.
16: Second curse: contempt for parents (mĕqallēh ʾāḇîw wĕʾimmô, "whoever dishonors/treats with contempt his father or mother"). The Fifth Commandment (Deuteronomy 5:16) commanded positive honor; this curse addresses its negative violation. Parents represent the intergenerational covenant line — to shame them is to tear the fabric of ancestral covenant transmission. The honor-shame culture of the ANE made parental contempt a social and religious offense of the first order.
17: Third curse: moving a neighbor's boundary marker (ʾăšer yasgîg gĕḇûl rēʿēhû). Land in Israel was covenant-allotted inheritance (naḥălâ), distributed by YHWH through the tribal allotment (Num 26; Josh 13–21). To move a boundary stone was to defraud both the neighbor and YHWH who distributed the land. Proverbs 22:28 and 23:10 repeat the prohibition. The Mesha Stele (Moabite king, ca. 840 BC) records land seizure from Israel, showing that boundary violations were a major ANE political and moral concern.
18: Fourth curse: misleading a blind person on the road. Leviticus 19:14 parallels this: "Do not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block in front of the blind." The blind person is a paradigmatic figure for all who are navigationally or informationally vulnerable — those who cannot verify what they are being told. The curse targets the exploitation of any form of disability or dependence. The principle extends across all situations where asymmetric knowledge creates power to deceive the vulnerable.
19: Fifth curse: denying justice to the foreigner (gēr), the fatherless (yātôm), or the widow (ʾalmānâ). This is the triad of the most vulnerable in ANE society — those without a male protector, without property rights, without legal standing in the patronage system. YHWH describes himself as the defender of this triad (Deuteronomy 10:18; Psalms 68:5). Judicial corruption targeting those with no family advocate is here elevated to a covenantal crime with divine sanction. The triad recurs throughout Deuteronomy: Deuteronomy 10:18; Deuteronomy 14:29; Deuteronomy 16:11, 14; Deuteronomy 24:17–21.
20: Sixth curse: sleeping with his father's wife (stepmother). "Violated his father's marriage" — literally "uncovered the corner/skirt of his father" (gillâ kĕnap̄ ʾāḇîw), an idiom for accessing someone under the father's protective covering (marriage garment). Reuben's act in Genesis 35:22 and Absalom's deliberate public act in 2 Samuel 16:22 (as political statement of usurpation) illustrate that this offense carried both sexual and political-honor dimensions. The son who violates the father's marriage asserts dominance over the household.
21: Seventh curse: bestiality (šōḵēḇ ʿim kol-bĕhēmâ, "whoever lies with any animal"). Also prohibited in Exodus 22:19, Leviticus 18:23, and Deuteronomy 20:15–16. The severity of covenant sanction signals the theological importance of the human-animal distinction in Israelite anthropology: humans bear the image of God (Genesis 1:26–27) and must not blur the boundary between kinds. ANE neighbors (Hittite law codes) also prohibited this, indicating a shared cultural aversion with distinct theological grounding in Israel.
22: Eighth curse: incest with sister — "whether she is his father's daughter or his mother's daughter." The curse explicitly covers both full and half-sisters. This closure of the half-sister loophole is significant: the patriarchal narrative includes Abraham marrying Sarah, his half-sister (Genesis 20:12). The Sinai covenant's prohibitions (Leviticus 18:9; Deuteronomy 20:17) and now Deuteronomy's curses formally supersede the norms of the patriarchal period. Covenant law governs the community's present practice, not ancestral precedent.
23: Ninth curse: sleeping with his mother-in-law (ḥōtantô). The relational violation destroys family integrity across two generations — the wife's generational line and the husband's marriage bond are both violated. The comprehensive reach of the sexual ethics section (Deuteronomy 27:20–23) covers spousal, sibling, and in-law relationships, systematically protecting the family structure that sustains the covenant community.
24: Tenth curse: secret attack on a neighbor (makkēh rēʿēhû bassāter, "whoever strikes his neighbor in secret"). Hidden violence against a neighbor, like the hidden idolatry of Deuteronomy 27:15, evades human courts. The public legal system could prosecute witnessed violence; covenantal curse fills the gap for attacks committed where no witness sees. The structural parallel between Deuteronomy 27:15 (secret idolatry) and Deuteronomy 27:24 (secret assault) shows the Dodecalogue's deliberate pattern: offenses against God and offenses against neighbor are subject to the same covenantal sanction.
25: Eleventh curse: accepting a bribe to kill an innocent person (šōḥaḏ lĕhakkôt nepeš dām nāqî, "bribe to strike/kill an innocent person"). Judicial murder through bribed violence is among the most corrosive social sins in the prophetic literature (Proverbs 17:23; Isaiah 1:23; Jeremiah 22:17; Micah 3:11; Ezekiel 22:12). It weaponizes the justice system against the innocent and destroys the social trust that covenant community requires. The specificity — bribed killing, not just bribery — targets the worst intersection of corruption and violence.
26: Twelfth curse — the summary curse: "Cursed is anyone who does not uphold the words of this law by carrying them out." The Dodecalogue ends not with a specific sin but with comprehensive covenant non-observance. This all-encompassing curse closes the section by placing the entire law under sanction: not just the eleven specific sins named above, but any failure to "uphold" (yāqîm, to establish, confirm, make stand) the law's full demands. Paul cites this verse in Galatians 3:10 ("Cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the Book of the Law") to establish that all who rely on the law are under its curse — the law's comprehensive reach is the basis for recognizing that only Christ, who bore the curse (Galatians 3:13, citing Deuteronomy 21:23), can deliver those whom the law condemns.
